TL;DR: This paper presents a method to enhance Biomedical Entity Linking (BEL) efficiency through instruction-tuning of generative models at the re-ranking stage, achieving notable accuracy and speed improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the computational inefficiency of Biomedical Entity Linking with large language models, making deployment in practical settings more feasible.

Method: The authors propose a set-wise instruction-tuning formulation applied during the re-ranking stage of the BEL pipeline, facilitating rapid and precise candidate selection.

Result: The method shows strong performance across multiple BEL benchmarks, improving linking accuracy by 3%-24% while also reducing inference time compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: Integration of the generative re-ranker into BeLink demonstrates the potential for practical applications of BEL by enhancing both accuracy and efficiency.

Abstract: Despite recent progress, Biomedical Entity Linking (BEL) with large language models (LLMs) remains computationally inefficient and challenging to deploy in practical settings. In this work, we demonstrate that instruction-tuning of open-source generative models can offer an effective solution when applied at the re-ranking stage of the BEL pipeline. We propose a set-wise instruction-tuning formulation that enables fast and accurate candidate selection. Our method demonstrates strong performance on multiple BEL benchmarks, yielding significant improvements in linking accuracy (3%-24%) while reducing inference time compared to the state-of-the-art. We integrate our generative re-ranker into BeLink, a modular, end-to-end system designed for practical real-world BEL applications.

</details>


### [150] [Does Slightly Mean Somewhat? Measuring Vague Intensity Words in LLM Numeric Actions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21827)
*Daniel Tabach*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study explores how language models interpret intensity words in numeric actions using a scale of degree modifiers. The findings highlight that these models compress intensity words into fewer outputs, exhibit state-dependent behavior, and lose ordinal distinctions near limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how well language models, like Claude Haiku, maintain the ordinal meanings of intensity words when required to produce numeric values based on natural language instructions.

Method: The study uses a controlled resource-allocation environment with 6,620 runs, varying intensity words and system states. It analyses the resulting numeric allocations and uses statistical tests like Spearman and Kruskal-Wallis to evaluate the findings.

Result: Findings indicate that intensity words are compressed into 5 median outputs despite having 10 original words. The model's outputs are highly influenced by the initial system state rather than the specific intensity word, especially near capacity limits.

Conclusion: The numeric interpretation of vague intensity words by the language model is compressed and state-dependent, lacking continuity as operational boundaries are approached.

Abstract: Do language models preserve the ordinal meaning of intensity words when those words must produce numeric actions? I study a researcher-constructed scale of 10 English degree modifiers, from slightly to drastically, informed by the Quirk et al. degree-modifier taxonomy, in a controlled resource-allocation environment where Claude Haiku receives a natural-language instruction, produces a numeric allocation, and a deterministic backend converts that allocation into a measurable outcome. The only variable that changes between runs is the intensity word or the starting system state, isolating their effects on the model's numeric output.
  Across 6,620 runs at T=0.0 and T=0.7, three patterns emerge. First, the model compresses 10 intensity words into 5 distinct median outputs: four lower-tier words all map to the same value, while stronger words break into higher regimes (Spearman rho = 0.845, p < 0.001). Second, when the current system state is supplied as context, separate Kruskal-Wallis tests show that grouping by starting allocation captures far more rank-based variance than grouping by word (epsilon-squared baseline = 0.782 vs. epsilon-squared word = 0.079), and lexical differentiation collapses to zero as the system approaches capacity. Third, near feasibility limits the model exhibits three behavioral modes: weak words hedge with small adjustments, strong words abstain entirely, and the word drastically pushes to the local ceiling. These patterns persist across temperature, with stochastic sampling broadening distributions but not restoring ordinal distinctions between words. In this model and domain, the model's numeric interpretation of vague intensity words is compressed, state-dependent, and discontinuous near operational boundaries.

</details>


### [151] [Comparing LLM and Fine-Tuned Model Performance on NVDRS Circumstance Extraction with Varying Prompt Complexity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21845)
*Geoffrey Martin,Xuan Zhong Feng,Yifan Peng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A study on improving the extraction of structured information from suicide-related death investigation narratives using a hybrid approach of language models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the understanding of circumstances leading to suicide by leveraging structured data from death narratives, which require semantic inference.

Method: Development of a 'Complexity Score' algorithm to improve prompt strategies and evaluation of LLMs against fine-tuned RoBERTa using data from NVDRS.

Result: LLMs outperform fine-tuned models on low-prevalence, complex inferential cases, and the framework generalizes well across various LLMs.

Conclusion: The proposed hybrid architecture effectively differentiates prompt strategies for rare, complex scenarios and common cases.

Abstract: Suicide is a leading cause of death in the United States, and understanding the circumstances that precede it requires extracting structured information from death investigation narratives. Many of these circumstances require semantic inference beyond simple keyword matching. We develop a ``Complexity Score'' algorithm that analyzes coding manual structure to predict when detailed prompts with full coding guidelines improve over name-only prompts. We then construct a hybrid approach that selects prompt strategy per circumstance. We evaluate large language models (LLMs) against fine-tuned RoBERTa on 25 inferentially complex circumstances from the National Violent Death Reporting System (NVDRS). We found that LLMs substantially outperform on low-prevalence circumstances where training data is insufficient. We further demonstrate that our framework generalizes across frontier LLMs, with GPT-5.2, Gemini 2.5 Pro and Llama-3 70B showing consistent performance patterns. These findings support a hybrid architecture where LLMs handle rare, inferentially complex circumstances while fine-tuned models handle common ones.

</details>


### [152] [One prompt is not enough: Instruction Sensitivity Undermines Embedding Model Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22544)
*Yevhen Kostiuk,Kenneth Enevoldsen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study highlights the issue of prompt sensitivity in instruction embedding models, revealing that single-prompt evaluations often misrepresent model performance and leaderboard rankings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitation of evaluating instruction embedding models with a single prompt, which neglects sensitivity to phrasing, leading to potentially misleading performance scores.

Method: An empirical analysis of prompt sensitivity across 6 embedding models, using 11 datasets and 15 task-specific prompts per dataset, totaling 990 evaluations.

Result: The analysis shows that default prompts can misrepresent performance, and leaderboard positions can be altered significantly based on prompt choices.

Conclusion: Single-prompt evaluations are inadequate for assessing instruction-tuned embedding models; benchmarks should include evaluations over multiple prompts or report sensitivity.

Abstract: Instruction embedding models have become common among state-of-the-art models, however are evaluated using a single prompt per task. The single-point evaluation ignores a main problem of the instruction-based approach namely: sensitivity to the phrasing of the instruction. We present an empirical study of prompt sensitivity across 6 embedding models, 11 datasets, and 15 task-specific prompts per dataset, a total of 990. We show that reported scores misrepresent the distribution of scores over plausible prompts. The default prompt can both systematically understate or overstate performance. Furthermore, we show that the leaderboard ranking is not robust to prompt selection: by choosing prompts favorably, any model in our study can be promoted to first place. Our findings suggest that single-prompt evaluation is insufficient for instruction-tuned embedding models and that benchmarks should incorporate prompt robustness, either by evaluating over multiple prompts or by reporting sensitivity alongside point estimates.

</details>


### [153] [ACC: Compiling Agent Trajectories for Long-Context Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21850)
*Qisheng Su,Zhen Fang,Shiting Huang,Yu Zeng,Yiming Zhao,Kou Shi,Ziao Zhang,Lin Chen,Zehui Chen,Lijun Wu,Feng Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Agent Context Compilation (ACC), a method to enhance long-context reasoning in LLMs by transforming agent trajectories into long-context QA pairs, enabling better training without the need for additional annotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of LLMs in long-context reasoning due to the ineffective use of scattered signals in standard agent training, necessitating a new methodology.

Method: ACC reformulates agent trajectories into long-context QA pairs that integrate multiple turns' responses and observations, making context dependencies explicit for training.

Result: Training with ACC yields significant improvements in long-range dependency modeling tasks like MRCR and GraphWalks, with Qwen3-30B-A3B achieving competitive results.

Conclusion: ACC is a scalable, effective approach for improving long-context reasoning in LLMs, facilitating efficient training while maintaining model capabilities.

Abstract: Recent development of agents has renewed demand for long-context reasoning capacity of LLMs. However, training LLMs for this capacity requires costly long-document curation or heuristic context synthesis. We observe that agents produce massive trajectories when solving problems, invoking tools and receiving environment observations across many turns. The evidence needed to answer the original question is thus scattered throughout these turns, requiring integration of distant context segments. Nevertheless, standard agent SFT masks tool responses and only trains turn-level tool selection, creating a supervision blind spot where these scattered signals go unused. We propose Agent Context Compilation (ACC), which converts trajectories from search, software engineering, and database querying agents into long-context QA pairs that combine the original question with tool responses and environment observations gathered across multiple turns, training the model to answer directly without tool use. This makes the dependencies between the question and the evidence explicit, enabling direct supervision of long-context reasoning over distant segments without additional annotation. ACC is a simple but effective approach that can be combined with any existing long-context extension or training method, providing scalable supervised fine-tuning data. We validate ACC on long-range dependency modeling tasks through MRCR and GraphWalks, challenging benchmarks requiring cross-turn coreference resolution and graph traversal over extended contexts. Training Qwen3-30B-A3B with ACC achieves 68.3 on MRCR (+18.1) and 77.5 on GraphWalks (+7.6), results comparable to Qwen3-235B-A22B, while preserving general capabilities on GPQA, MMLU-Pro, AIME, and IFEval. Further mechanism analysis reveals that the ACC-trained model exhibits task-adaptive attention restructuring and expert specialization.

</details>


### [154] [Hypergraph as Language](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21858)
*Mengqi Lei,Guohuan Xie,Shihui Ying,Shaoyi Du,Jun-Hai Yong,Siqi Li,Yue Gao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents Hyper-Align, a hypergraph-native alignment framework that improves large language models' (LLMs) ability to process high-order relational structures by utilizing hypergraphs rather than traditional pairwise graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the limitations of existing graph-centric methods in modeling complex, high-order relational structures present in real-world scenarios.

Method: Hyper-Align utilizes a new hypergraph-native alignment approach, employing a Hypergraph Incidence Detail Template with Overview (HIDT-O) for encoding hypergraphs, alongside a Hypergraph Incidence Projector (HIP) for mapping hypergraph structures to LLM token space.

Result: Experimental evaluations demonstrate that Hyper-Align outperforms traditional methods in both in-domain and zero-shot tasks, showcasing its effectiveness in handling hypergraph structures.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a significant advancement in the application of LLMs to complex relational patterns and sets a new benchmark for hypergraph structural modeling.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently shown strong potential in modeling relational structures. However, existing approaches remain fundamentally graph-centric: they focus on processing pairwise graph structures into tokens that LLMs can understand. In contrast, many real-world relational patterns do not naturally conform to the pairwise-edge assumption, and are better modeled as high-order associations in hypergraphs. For hypergraph structures, existing methods often fail to preserve the native semantics that multiple objects are jointly connected by the same high-order relation, limiting their ability to exploit complex structures. To address this limitation, we put forth the "Hypergraph as Language" perspective and propose Hyper-Align, a hypergraph-native alignment framework for large language models. Hyper-Align compiles the query-object-centered hypergraph context into hypergraph tokens directly consumable by a base LLM. Specifically, we introduce Hypergraph Incidence Detail Template with Overview (HIDT-O), which serializes high-order association structures into a fixed-shape hybrid template combining local incidence details and overview-level summaries. We then design a Hypergraph Incidence Projector (HIP), which maps native high-order incidence structures into the LLM token space through explicit semantic-structural decoupling and bidirectional message passing between vertices and hyperedges. We further define a concrete Hypergraph-as-Language input protocol, which jointly feeds hypergraph tokens and textual prompts into a frozen base LLM, supporting both vertex-level and hyperedge-level tasks under a unified question-answering paradigm. To systematically evaluate different methods in hypergraph structural modeling, we introduce HyperAlign-Bench. Extensive experiments show that Hyper-Align significantly outperforms existing methods across in-domain and zero-shot evaluations.

</details>


### [155] [Token-weighted Direct Preference Optimization with Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21883)
*Chengyu Huang,Zhuohang Li,Sheng-Yen Chou,Claire Cardie*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Token-weighted DPO (TwDPO) and AttentionPO, a new training approach that improves Large Language Models' alignment with human preferences by using content-aware, token-weighted evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance Large Language Models' alignment with human preferences without the drawbacks of previous token optimization methods that lack robustness and increase training costs.

Method: The paper presents Token-weighted DPO (TwDPO) and its application, AttentionPO, which estimates token weights using the LLM's attention mechanism, enabling the model to evaluate its responses based on content relevance.

Result: AttentionPO significantly outperforms existing Preference Optimization methods on benchmarks such as AlpacaEval, MT-Bench, and ArenaHard, demonstrating its effectiveness in aligning LLMs with human preferences.

Conclusion: AttentionPO provides a more efficient and content-aware method for token weighting in preference optimization, enhancing the overall performance of Large Language Models.

Abstract: Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) aligns Large Language Models with human preferences without the need for a separate reward model. However, DPO treats all tokens in responses equally, neglecting the differing importance of individual tokens. Existing token-level PO methods compute the token weights using either token-position-based heuristic functions or probability estimates given by a separately trained model, which lacks robustness and incurs extra training cost. In contrast, we propose Token-weighted DPO (TwDPO) -- a novel training objective grounded on token-weighted RL -- and AttentionPO -- an instantiation of TwDPO that uses attention from the LLM itself to estimate token weights. AttentionPO prompts the LLM to serve as a pairwise judge and check where the model attends when comparing the responses. This design makes AttentionPO content-aware, adjusting weights based on response content, and efficient, incurring only two extra forward passes per example. Experiment results show that AttentionPO significantly improves performance on AlpacaEval, MT-Bench, and ArenaHard, surpassing existing Preference Optimization methods.

</details>


### [156] [Claim-Selective Certification for High-Risk Medical Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21949)
*Shao Kan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a claim-selective certification approach for evaluating Medical RAG systems in high-risk QA settings, focusing on decomposing responses into verifiable claims and scoring them against retrieved evidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the evaluation of Medical RAG systems beyond a binary decision, addressing the complexity of mixed evidence in high-stakes scenarios.

Method: The proposed method involves decomposing responses into verifiable claims, scoring these claims against retrieved evidence, and categorizing responses into full, partial, conflict, or abstain using an intent-aware selector.

Result: The system achieved excellent performance metrics on both development (UCCR=0.0000, PAU=1.0000, action accuracy=0.9204) and test sets (UCCR=0.0000, PAU=0.9967, action accuracy=0.8997), highlighting its effectiveness in claim certification.

Conclusion: The interface developed allows for a clear distinction between action-label predictions and evidence-driven claim selection in contexts with mixed evidence.

Abstract: Medical RAG systems in high-risk QA settings are often evaluated through a single answer-or-abstain decision, but mixed evidence may support one claim, require conditions for another, and contradict a third. We study claim-selective certification: each response is decomposed into verifiable claims, scored against retrieved evidence, and mapped by an intent-aware selector to {full, partial, conflict, abstain}. On the primary weak-label certificate protocol, whose real-source-only dev/test rows cover the naturally occurring non-abstain actions, the full system records UCCR=0.0000, PAU=1.0000, PAU Precision=0.9901, and action accuracy=0.9204 on dev (n=314), and UCCR=0.0000, PAU=0.9967, PAU Precision=0.9739, and action accuracy=0.8997 on test (n=319). UCCR measures unsupported-claim risk within the certificate definition, and a source-missing counterfactual slice evaluates abstain under empty evidence. Shortcut controls quantify the action-label prior explained by source and intent metadata, while source/evidence-novel slices characterize transfer boundaries. The resulting interface separates action-label prediction from evidence-linked claim selection under mixed evidence.

</details>


### [157] [Diagnosis Is Not Prescription: Linguistic Co-Adaptation Explains Patching Hazards in LLM Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21958)
*Yoon Jeonghun,Kim Dongchan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Diagnostic Paradox in multi-module LLM agents, where the routing module is identified as a failure bottleneck, yet correcting it can degrade performance. Instead, patching an upstream query-rewriting module improves outcomes, supported by empirical evidence and the Linguistic Contract hypothesis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate the effectiveness of intervention strategies in multi-module LLM agents, particularly the paradoxical performance impacts of correcting the routing module compared to an upstream module.

Method: Empirical analysis of three independent agent families, focusing on the effects of intervention strategies such as prompt-level corrections, and the introduction of a co-adaptation measure to assess the alignment of module errors.

Result: While the routing module is identified as a primary bottleneck, corrections there often degrade performance; however, interventions on upstream query-rewriting modules improve outcomes significantly, with statistical support across various measures.

Conclusion: The paper supports the Linguistic Contract hypothesis, showing that upstream corrections maintain alignment with downstream modules, whereas direct corrections to the routing module disrupt this alignment, confirming a consistent trend across agent families.

Abstract: When a multi-module LLM agent fails, the module most responsible for the failure is not necessarily the best place to intervene. We demonstrate this Diagnostic Paradox empirically: causal analysis consistently identifies the routing module -- which selects which tool to call next -- as the primary bottleneck across three independent agent families. Yet injecting prompt-level correction examples into this module consistently degrades performance, sometimes severely. Patching an upstream query-rewriting module instead reliably improves outcomes. The effect holds with statistical significance on two agent families and directional consistency on a third; alternative repair strategies at the routing module (instruction rewriting, model upgrade) are neutral, confirming that the harm is specific to correction-injection patching.
  We explain this asymmetry through the Linguistic Contract hypothesis: each downstream module implicitly adapts to its upstream's characteristic error distribution, so correcting the bottleneck breaks this implicit alignment in a way that upstream corrections do not. We operationalize this via a per-agent co-adaptation measure, derived from diagnosis alone, and show it is consistently associated with patching harm across agent families: higher co-adaptation co-occurs with harm, lower with safety. This trend holds across all three agent families, providing preliminary support for the hypothesis beyond a single-agent observation.

</details>


### [158] [SpecHop: Continuous Speculation for Accelerating Multi-Hop Retrieval Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.21965)
*Mehrdad Saberi,Keivan Rezaei,Soheil Feizi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SpecHop accelerates multi-hop tool use in language models, reducing latency by up to 40% without sacrificing accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enhance the efficiency of language models using external tools for information-heavy tasks, overcoming latency issues associated with multi-hop tool use.

Method: Introduces SpecHop, a continuous speculation framework that manages multiple threads, asynchronously verifies predictions, and commits or rolls back based on the accuracy of tool outputs during complex tasks.

Result: SpecHop achieves significant latency reductions, showing empirical reductions of up to 40% on multi-hop retrieval-augmented tasks, while theoretically aligning with predicted latency gains.

Conclusion: SpecHop demonstrates that it is possible to reduce latency in language model tool use without compromising accuracy, with the potential for near-oracle performance given sufficient threads.

Abstract: Large language models increasingly use external tools such as web search and document retrieval to solve information-intensive tasks. However, multi-hop tool use in complex tasks introduces substantial latency, since the model must repeatedly wait for tool observations before continuing. We study how to accelerate such trajectories without changing the final trajectory the model would have taken without acceleration, assuming access to faster but less reliable speculator tools. We develop a theoretical framework for lossless speculation in multi-hop tool-use settings, characterizing the optimal achievable latency gain. We propose SpecHop, a continuous speculation framework that maintains multiple speculative threads, verifies predicted observations asynchronously as target tool outputs arrive, commits correct branches, and rolls back incorrect ones. This preserves accuracy while reducing wall-clock latency. We show that SpecHop can approach oracle latency gains with enough active threads. Empirically, on retrieval-augmented multi-hop tasks, SpecHop closely matches theoretical predictions and reduces latency by up to 40\% in some settings. Code: https://github.com/mehrdadsaberi/spechop

</details>


### [159] [Hallucination as Commitment Failure: Larger LLMs Misfire Despite Knowing the Answer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22007)
*Jewon Yeom,Jaewon Sok,Heejun Kim,Seonghyeon Park,Jeongjae Park,Taesup Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper explores the relationship between model scale, hallucination frequency, and the distribution of probability across answer concepts, revealing a mechanism where instruction tuning sharpens answer commitment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the assumption that hallucination in models arises solely from the absence of the correct answer in their generation-time distribution.

Method: The study introduces a semantic approach to evaluate answer availability by analyzing token-level variants and probability distributions in Qwen and Llama models of varying sizes (0.8B to 72B).

Result: Findings show that 16-47% of Instruct model hallucinations occur even when the correct concept is already represented with probability mass, indicating that model scale impacts hallucination rates; the probability mass is more dispersed in hallucinations than in correct responses.

Conclusion: Instruction tuning at scale enhances answer commitment, which links the helpfulness of responses and the tendency to hallucinate, suggesting they stem from the same mechanism.

Abstract: Hallucination is often viewed as a direct consequence of missing knowledge: a model answers incorrectly when the correct answer is absent from its generation-time distribution, and correctly when it is present. We test this assumption by introducing a semantic notion of answer availability that aggregates token-level variants expressing the same answer concept, and asks whether the correct concept is already available at the moment the model commits to an answer. Across Qwen and Llama models from 0.8B to 72B in both Instruct and Base variants, 16-47% of Instruct hallucinations occur with substantial probability mass already on the correct concept, and the rate rises monotonically with scale. Comparing such failures against correct generations with matched semantic support, the distinguishing factor is not whether the correct concept is represented, but how its probability is distributed: correct generations concentrate mass on a single surface form, hallucinations disperse it across alternatives. The same sharpening asymmetry extends across multi-token generation and is detectable in pre-generation hidden states. Together, these results identify a single mechanism: instruction tuning sharpens answer commitment with scale, making helpfulness and confident hallucination two consequences of the same underlying disposition.

</details>


### [160] [LatentOmni: Rethinking Omni-Modal Understanding via Unified Audio-Visual Latent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22012)
*Yifan Dai,Zhenhua Wu,Bohan Zeng,Daili Hua,Jialing Liu,Bozhou Li,Yuran Wang,Chengzhuo Tong,Hao Liang,Xiaochen Ma,Junbo Niu,Tianyu Guo,Yang Shi,Yue Ding,Yiyan Ji,Bingyin Mei,Yushuo Guan,Yuanxing Zhang,Pengfei Wan,Fangcheng Fu,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LatentOmni is a cross-modal reasoning framework that improves audio-visual reasoning by using a unified latent space, enhancing temporal consistency and sensory feature alignment, resulting in superior performance in multimodal tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with fine-grained audio-visual reasoning due to limitations in processing continuous signals, which are often compressed into discrete tokens that hinder temporal grounding.

Method: LatentOmni employs a unified latent space for reasoning, interleaves textual and audio-visual latents, introduces feature-level supervision, and uses Omni-Sync Position Embedding to align temporal states effectively.

Result: LatentOmni outperforms existing open-source models and the Explicit Text CoT baseline across several audio-visual reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating enhanced joint reasoning capabilities.

Conclusion: Latent-space joint reasoning presents a promising approach for advancing omnimodal understanding in AI.

Abstract: Joint audio-visual reasoning is essential for omnimodal understanding, yet current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still struggle when reasoning requires fine-grained evidence from both modalities. A central limitation is that explicit text-based chain-of-thought (CoT) compresses continuous audio-visual signals into discrete tokens, weakening temporal grounding and shifting intermediate reasoning toward language priors. We argue that a unified latent space is a better medium for such reasoning because it preserves dense sensory information while remaining compatible with autoregressive generation. Based on this insight, we propose \textbf{LatentOmni}, a cross-modal reasoning framework that interleaves textual reasoning with audio-visual latent states. LatentOmni introduces feature-level supervision to align latent reasoning states with task-relevant sensory features and uses Omni-Sync Position Embedding (OSPE) to maintain temporal consistency between latent audio and visual states. We further construct \textbf{LatentOmni-Instruct-35K}, a dataset of audio-visual interleaved reasoning trajectories for supervising latent-space reasoning. Comprehensive evaluation across multiple audio-visual reasoning benchmarks demonstrates that LatentOmni achieves the best performance among the evaluated open-source models and consistently outperforms the Explicit Text CoT baseline, supporting latent-space joint reasoning as a promising path toward stronger omnimodal understanding.

</details>


### [161] [FlyRoute: Self-Evolving Agent Profiling via Data Flywheel for Adaptive Task Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22057)
*Rongjun Li,Ziyu Zhou,Yihang Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FlyRoute is a self-evolving profiling framework that dynamically updates agent capabilities using real traffic data, enhancing LLM router performance from 72.57% to 89.83% accuracy through targeted data-efficient exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current enterprise routers fail to adapt profiles dynamically as agent capabilities evolve, leading to outdated query assignments.

Method: FlyRoute collects performance evidence from real traffic, uses a targeted exploration policy to focus on under-profiled agents, and updates learned capability descriptions for improved routing accuracy.

Result: FlyRoute enhances routing accuracy from 72.57% to 89.83% after processing 7,211 labeled queries, showing significant improvement in performance and efficiency with minimal seed queries.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that targeted evidence retrieval and dynamic profile updates significantly improve the performance of LLM routers, particularly in cold-start scenarios.

Abstract: Enterprise routers assign queries to expert agents, yet deployed profiles stay static while agents evolve (prompts, tools, models), and developers rarely keep descriptions or exemplars current. We present FlyRoute, a self-evolving profiling framework that grows capability evidence from real traffic: dispatch candidates, quality-gate successful pairs into each agent's success store, periodically distill evidence into learned capability descriptions, and inject those descriptions together with BM25-retrieved successes into an LLM router. To make this flywheel data-efficient, FlyRoute introduces a targeted exploration policy that combines profile uncertainty, BM25 relevance, and lexical novelty, prioritizing under-profiled agents only for plausible queries and avoiding redundant evidence collection. In experiments on our proprietary enterprise developer-support dataset of real routed queries, FlyRoute improves a same-backbone zero-shot LLM router from 72.57% to 78.04% with only five seed queries per agent, showing that profile retrieval already strengthens cold-start routing. After streaming 7,211 labeled training queries through the flywheel, accuracy rises to 89.83% (+17.26pp over zero-shot; +11.79pp over cold start), with consistent gains across four expert domains under standard routing accuracy on single-gold test queries.

</details>


### [162] [Hy-MT2: A Family of Fast, Efficient and Powerful Multilingual Translation Models in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22064)
*Mao Zheng,Zheng Li,Tao Chen,Bo Lv,Mingrui Sun,Mingyang Song,Jinlong Song,Hong Huang,Decheng Wu,Hai Wang,Yifan Song,Yanfeng Chen,Guanwei Zhang,Guanghua Yu,Yi Su,Hong Liu,Jinxiang Ou,Keyao Wang,Weile Chen,Haozhao Kuang,Kai Wang,Nuo Chen,Zihao Zheng,Chenhao Wang,Bin Xing,Chengcheng Xu,Tinghao Yu,Binghong Wu,Long Xu,Jiacheng Shi,Yunhao Wang,Baifang Chen,Lei Zhang,Qi Yang,Zhao Wu,Jiacheng Li,Lan Jiang,Lanrui Wang,Kai Zhang,Shuaipeng Li,Zhongzhi Chen,Weixuan Sun,Jiaqi Zhu,An Wang,Wei Li,Jun Xia,Weidong Han,Wutian Yang,Litong Hui,Luoguo Jia,Jiajia Wu,Xinpeng Zhou,Tianxiang Fei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Hy-MT2 is a versatile multilingual translation model family with various sizes optimized for performance and efficiency, excelling in multiple evaluation scenarios and outperforming competitors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The increasing demand for efficient translation in diverse contexts necessitates advanced multilingual models that can operate swiftly and effectively in real-world applications.

Method: Hy-MT2 includes three model sizes (1.8B, 7B, and 30B-A3B with MoE) and utilizes AngelSlim for extreme quantization to optimize storage and inference speed.

Result: The models support 33 languages and demonstrate superior performance in various translation tasks, outperforming both open-source models and mainstream APIs.

Conclusion: Hy-MT2 provides a highly efficient and capable solution for multilingual translation, significantly enhancing performance in multiple scenarios.

Abstract: Hy-MT2 is a family of fast-thinking multilingual translation models designed for complex real-world scenarios. It includes three model sizes: 1.8B, 7B, and 30B-A3B (MoE), all of which support translation among 33 languages and effectively follow translation instructions in multiple languages. For on-device deployment, with AngelSlim 1.25-bit extreme quantization, the 1.8B model requires only 440 MB of storage and improves inference speed by 1.5x. Multi-dimensional evaluations show that Hy-MT2 delivers outstanding performance across general, real-world business, domain-specific, and instruction-following translation tasks. The 7B and 30B models outperform open-source models such as DeepSeek-V4-Pro and Kimi K2.6 in fast-thinking mode, while the lightweight 1.8B model also surpasses mainstream commercial APIs from providers such as Microsoft and Doubao overall.

</details>


### [163] [Faithful-MR1: Faithful Multimodal Reasoning via Anchoring and Reinforcing Visual Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22072)
*Changyuan Tian,Zhicong Lu,Huaxing Liu,Xiang Wang,Shuai Li,Yu Chen,Wenqian Lv,Zichuan Lin,Juncheng Diao,Deheng Ye*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Faithful-MR1 improves multimodal reasoning in language models by enhancing visual attention and addressing gaps in perception and reasoning through a structured training framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The research aims to tackle the faithfulness challenge in multimodal models, particularly the disconnect between perceived visual evidence and its use in reasoning.

Method: The Faithful-MR1 framework consists of two stages: 1) Anchoring, which supervises attention on image regions instead of textual descriptions, and 2) Reinforcing, which applies counterfactual interventions to incentivize the use of relevant visual evidence during reasoning.

Result: Faithful-MR1 demonstrates superior performance on multimodal reasoning benchmarks compared to existing baselines, while requiring less training data.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the perception-reasoning disconnect, resulting in improved performance in multimodal reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for advancing complex reasoning in large language models, and recent work extends RLVR to multimodal large language models (MLLMs). This transfer, however, surfaces a faithfulness challenge: faithful perception of task-relevant visual evidence and faithful use of that evidence during reasoning, leading to unsatisfactory gains on multimodal benchmarks. Specifically, existing perception supervision often operates on textual descriptions rather than natively on image regions, and faithful use is largely overlooked, exposing the perception-reasoning disconnect where correctly perceived evidence is dropped or contradicted during reasoning. To close these gaps, we propose Faithful-MR1, a training framework that anchors and reinforces visual attention to address both halves of faithful multimodal reasoning. The Anchoring stage turns perception into an explicit pre-reasoning subtask, supervising a dedicated <Focus> token's attention directly against image regions rather than through textual descriptions. The Reinforcing stage exposes faithful use through counterfactual image intervention, rewarding answer-correct trajectories that concentrate visual attention where vision causally matters. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Faithful-MR1 outperforms recent multimodal reasoning baselines on both Qwen2.5-VL-Instruct 3B and 7B backbones while using substantially less training data.

</details>


### [164] [ArabDiscrim: A Decade-Long Arabic Facebook Corpus on Racism and Discrimination](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22081)
*Wajdi Zaghouani,Shimaa Amer Ibrahim,Mabrouka Bessghaier,Houda Bouamor*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduction of ArabDiscrim, a comprehensive Arabic dataset for studying racism and discrimination on Facebook, integrating user engagement metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a rich resource for analyzing racism and discrimination in Arabic social media, addressing gaps in existing datasets.

Method: Compilation of 293K Arabic Facebook posts, including curated lexical terms related to racism and discrimination, along with user engagement data and attribution patterns.

Result: Creation of a unique dataset that enhances the understanding of language and audience reactions, promoting fairness in Arabic natural language processing (NLP).

Conclusion: ArabDiscrim serves as a pivotal resource for researchers focusing on language, ethics, and social dynamics in Arabic online environments.

Abstract: We present ArabDiscrim, a decade-long lexical resource and corpus of 293K public Arabic Facebook posts (2014--2024) discussing racism and discrimination. Unlike existing Twitter-centric datasets, ArabDiscrim integrates platform-native engagement signals, including reactions, shares, comments, and page metadata, enabling joint analysis of language and audience response. The resource includes 200 curated terms (100 racism-related and 100 discrimination-related) with morphological regex families (13+ inflections per lemma), and 20 discrimination axes capturing identity-based grounds for unequal treatment. It also provides explicit attribution patterns. Released under a restricted research-use license for ethical compliance with platform terms, ArabDiscrim supports weak supervision, axis-aware sampling, and platform ecology research. By bridging lexical depth and ecological validity, it establishes a foundation for fairness-oriented, platform-aware Arabic NLP.

</details>


### [165] [SynAE: A Framework for Measuring the Quality of Synthetic Data for Tool-Calling Agent Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22564)
*Shuaiqi Wang,Aadyaa Maddi,Zinan Lin,Giulia Fanti*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SynAE is an evaluation framework designed to assess the quality of synthetic datasets used for testing tool-calling agents, addressing limitations of real datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for effective evaluation of tool-calling agents due to limitations of real datasets, such as sensitivity and sparsity, drives the use of synthetic datasets.

Method: SynAE evaluates synthetic datasets through metrics related to task instructions, tool calls, final outputs, and overall diversity, highlighting the importance of a multi-axis evaluation.

Result: SynAE reveals fine-grained variations in data quality and shows that no single metric can fully characterize synthetic data, leading to a comprehensive evaluation methodology.

Conclusion: The framework emphasizes the necessity for multi-axis evaluation of synthetic data in agent testing to improve quality and reliability.

Abstract: Today, tool-calling agents are commonly evaluated or tested on static datasets of execution traces, including input commands, agent responses, and associated tool calls. However, internal production datasets are often insufficient or unusable for testing; for example, they may contain sensitive or proprietary data, or they may be too sparse to support comprehensive testing (especially pre-deployment). In these settings, practitioners are increasingly replacing or augmenting real datasets with synthetic ones for evaluation purposes. A key challenge is quantifying the relation between these synthetic datasets and the real data. We introduce SynAE, an evaluation framework for assessing how well synthetic benchmarks for multi-turn, tool-calling agents replicate and augment the characteristics of real data trajectories. SynAE assesses the validity, fidelity, and diversity of synthetic data across four metric categories: (i) task instructions and intermediate responses, (ii) tool calls, (iii) final outputs, and (iv) downstream evaluation. We evaluate SynAE using recent agent benchmarks and test common synthetic data failure modes via realistic and controlled generation schemes. SynAE detects fine-grained variations in data validity, fidelity and diversity, and shows that no single metric is sufficient to fully characterize synthetic data quality, motivating a multi-axis evaluation of synthetic data for agent testing. A demo of SynAE is available at https://synae-2026-synae-demo.static.hf.space/index.html, with code at https://github.com/wsqwsq/SynAE.

</details>


### [166] [A Comparative Study of Language Models for Khmer Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22099)
*Sereiwathna Ros,Phannet Pov,Ratanaktepi Chhor,Kimleang Ly,Wan-Sup Cho,Saksonita Khoeurn*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) system for Khmer-language telecom documents, identifying BGE-M3 as the top retriever and comparing multiple generator models across various performance metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate the effectiveness of RAG for low-resource, non-Latin-script languages, specifically Khmer, and improve factual accuracy in question answering systems.

Method: The study benchmarks three embedding models for dense retrieval and evaluates five generator backends using RAGAS-inspired performance metrics on a dataset of Khmer question-answer pairs.

Result: BGE-M3 outperforms other retrievers in terms of several metrics, while no single generator model excels across all metrics, with Qwen3.5 showing high faithfulness and context relevance.

Conclusion: The choice of retriever is critical for Khmer RAG systems, and generator performance is contingent on specific evaluation priorities such as grounding or factual accuracy.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising paradigm for grounding large language model (LLM) outputs in retrieved evidence, thereby reducing hallucination and improving factual accuracy. Its efficacy, however, remains largely unexamined for low-resource, non-Latin-script languages such as Khmer. In this paper, we present a RAG-based question answering system for Khmer-language telecom-domain documents. We conduct a two-phase comparative evaluation. First, we benchmark three embedding models: BGE-M3 (567M), Jina-Embeddings-v3 (570M), and Qwen3-Embedding (597M), for dense retrieval over Khmer documents. BGE-M3 consistently performs best, achieving a Hit Rate@3 of 0.285, File Hit Rate@3 of 0.700, MRR@3 of 0.221, and Precision@3 of 0.112, substantially outperforming the other retrievers. Second, using BGE-M3 as the selected retriever, we evaluate five generator backends: Qwen3 (8B), Qwen3.5 (9B), Sailor2-8B-Chat, SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat, and Llama-SEA-LION-v2-8B-IT, on a curated golden dataset of 200 Khmer question-answer pairs. To quantify system performance, we apply six RAGAS-inspired metrics: faithfulness, answer relevance, context relevance, factual correctness, answer similarity, and answer correctness. The results show no single model dominates across all metrics: Qwen3.5-9B achieves the highest faithfulness (0.859) and context relevance (0.726), Qwen3-8B attains the highest factual correctness (0.380), and SeaLLMs-v3-7B-Chat performs best on answer relevance (0.867), answer similarity (0.836), and answer correctness (0.599). These findings highlight that retriever choice remains a major bottleneck for Khmer RAG, while generator strengths vary depending on whether the priority is grounding, factual precision, or semantic similarity.

</details>


### [167] [Cross-Lingual Consensus: Aligning Multilingual Cultural Knowledge via Multilingual Self-Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22137)
*Andrew Ivan Soegeng,Patrick Sutanto,Tan Sang Nguyen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a self-supervised framework to improve the cultural performance of LLMs across languages by leveraging multilingual self-consistency and self-critique mechanisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the performance discrepancies of LLMs across languages and the Western-centric bias when prompted in English, aiming for better cultural representation.

Method: The proposed method utilizes multilingual self-consistency to collect reliable cultural responses and a self-critique mechanism to enhance knowledge transfer to weaker languages.

Result: Evaluations on the BLEnD benchmark showed an average improvement of 5.03% in cultural alignment for English queries, based entirely on self-generated data.

Conclusion: The study concludes that latent cultural knowledge can be activated and shared across languages, resulting in LLMs that are more culturally equitable and consistent.

Abstract: Although Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate strong capabilities across various tasks, they exhibit significant performance discrepancies across languages. While prompting LLMs in English typically yields the highest general performance, it often induces a Western-centric bias, hindering the model's ability to accurately reflect diverse cultural knowledge. We hypothesize that LLMs already possess rich cultural knowledge embedded within local-language representations, but fail to retrieve it when prompted in English. To bridge this cross-lingual knowledge gap, we propose a novel self-supervised framework. Our method leverages multilingual self-consistency to identify the most reliable cultural responses across languages, combined with a self-critique mechanism to transfer this knowledge to the weaker language. Evaluations on the BLEnD benchmark demonstrate that our approach significantly improves cultural alignment-boosting performance on English queries by an average of 5.03%-relying entirely on self-generated data. Ultimately, our work demonstrates that latent cultural knowledge can be successfully surfaced and propagated across languages, enabling more culturally equitable and consistent LLMs.

</details>


### [168] [Psy-Chronicle:A Structured Pipeline for Synthesizing Long-Horizon Campus Psychological Counseling Dialogues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22140)
*Chaogui Gou,Jiarui Liang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents Psy-Chronicle, a framework for generating long-horizon dialogues in college psychological counseling, addressing the limitations of existing short-interaction data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing psychological counseling data mainly focuses on short interactions, which hinders the understanding of how psychological distress evolves over time in college students.

Method: Psy-Chronicle uses a temporal stress event graph to model campus stress events and employs interactive simulation between student and counselor agents, along with a structured memory integration mechanism to create continuous long-horizon dialogues.

Result: CPCD, a Chinese dataset, was created with 100 student profiles and 90,000 dialogues. CPCD-Bench was developed to assess long-horizon counseling capabilities across session-level response, memory recall, and reasoning dimensions, showing effective improvements in response generation and memory recall but limited advancements in reasoning.

Conclusion: The study highlights challenges in organizing event-chains and causal explanations, suggesting they are key areas for further research in long-horizon psychological counseling.

Abstract: In recent years, large language models have shown substantial potential in psychological support tasks. However, existing psychological counseling data mostly rely on single-turn question answering or short multi-turn dialogues, making it difficult to characterize how college students' psychological distress accumulates, interacts, and gradually evolves over long periods within campus life events. To address this issue, this paper proposes Psy-Chronicle, a structured data-generation framework for synthesizing long-horizon campus psychological counseling dialogues. We generate a semester-spanning temporal stress event graph to model the chronological order and evolutionary dependencies among campus stress events. Through interactive simulation between a student agent and a counselor agent, together with a structured memory integration mechanism, Psy-Chronicle generates long-horizon dialogues with continuity across counseling sessions. Based on Psy-Chronicle, we construct and open-source CPCD, a Chinese long-horizon dialogue dataset for college psychological counseling, containing 100 student profiles, 90,000 counseling dialogues. We further build CPCD-Bench to evaluate models' long-horizon campus counseling capabilities from three dimensions: session-level response, long-horizon memory recall, and temporal-causal reasoning. Experimental results show that CPCD effectively improves session-level response generation and long-horizon memory recall for models with the same base architecture. Meanwhile, improvements in temporal-causal reasoning remain limited, indicating that event-chain organization and causal explanation are key challenges in long-horizon psychological counseling modeling. The related code and data are available at: https://github.com/EdwinUSTB/Psy-Chronicle

</details>


### [169] [Do Factual Recall Mechanisms Carry over from Text to Speech in Multimodal Language Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22170)
*Luca Modica,Filip Landin,Mehrdad Farahani,Livia Qian,Gabriel Skantze,Richard Johansson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates the differences in how Speech Language Models encode, store, and retrieve factual knowledge in text and speech modalities, revealing that mechanisms for factual recall are partially different between them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the similarities and differences in model-internal mechanisms when processing text and speech in Speech Language Models, particularly focusing on factual knowledge management.

Method: Causal Mediation Analysis is employed to analyze the storage and recall of factual associations in Speech Language Models, specifically using SpiritLM, a multimodal model that integrates discrete speech tokens.

Result: Initial findings indicate discrepancies in factual recall between text-to-text and speech-to-text modalities, suggesting that mechanisms for factual recall are not entirely consistent across modalities.

Conclusion: The study enhances the understanding of how factual associations are encoded in Speech Language Models and provides insights for the development of better speech-enabled AI systems.

Abstract: In recent years, several Speech Language Models (SLMs) that represent speech and written text jointly have been presented. The question then emerges about how model-internal mechanisms are similar and different when operating in the two modalities. We focus on how these systems encode, store, and retrieve factual knowledge, which has previously been investigated for text-only models. To investigate mechanisms behind the storage and recall of factual association in SLMs, we leverage Causal Mediation Analysis, a technique previously applied to text-based models.
  Initial results using SpiritLM, a multimodal model integrating discrete speech tokens reveal discrepancies between text-to-text and speech-to-text results, suggesting that the emergent mechanisms for factual recall are only partially carried over from the text to the speech modality. These results advance our understanding of how internal mechanisms encode factual associations in SLMs while contributing insights for improving speech-enabled AI systems.

</details>


### [170] [Structure Retention in Embedding Spaces as a Predictor of Benchmark Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22202)
*Amanda Myntti,Jenna Kanerva,Veronika Laippala,Filip Ginter*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper analyzes the organization of embedding spaces in high-performing models across various tasks and languages, finding strong correlations between embedding characteristics and model performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how embedding models organize their spaces and how this organization relates to performance on various tasks.

Method: Evaluated 25 embedding models across five MTEB tasks, measuring nearest-neighbor overlap and magnitude differences in ICA between paired text instances, in both English and multilingual settings.

Result: Found strong correlations (up to 0.97) between embedding characteristics and task performance, indicating the degree of linearity and information retention in embeddings.

Conclusion: The study enhances the understanding of embeddings and suggests implications for future training objectives and conditional embedding optimization.

Abstract: In this paper, we show that high-performing embedding models organize their embedding spaces in a consistent way. We evaluate 25 contemporary embedding models on five MTEB tasks spanning four diverse task categories (retrieval, bitext mining, pair classification, and summarization) in both English and multilingual settings, and reveal that nearest-neighbor overlap and magnitude differences in independent component analysis (ICA) between paired text instances strongly correlate (even up to 0.97) with performance on the given task. Ultimately, we show that embedding tasks display varying degrees of linearity and reliance on retention of local information. Our results further the understanding of embeddings, their relation to model performance, and shed light on possible future training objectives and optimizing conditional embeddings.

</details>


### [171] [Evaluation of Chunking Strategies for Effective Text Embedding in Low-Resource Language on Agricultural Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22203)
*Sovandara Chhoun,Pichdara Po,Sereiwathna Ros,Wan-Sup Cho,Saksonita Khoeurn*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The study compares four text chunking methods in a RAG framework for Khmer agricultural documents, finding the Recursive method most effective in performance metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The study aims to improve dense retrieval methods for Khmer agricultural documents, considering the unique challenges posed by the Khmer language's morphology.

Method: Four chunking methods (Recursive, Khmer-Aware, Sentence-Based, LLM-Based) were evaluated using a retrieval system with document encoding via the BGE-M3 model and retrieval through FAISS, assessing performance through metrics like L2 distance and relevance.

Result: The Recursive chunking method outperformed others, achieving the lowest L2 distance and highest scores in Answer Relevance and Khmer IoU, with significant differences noted against the Sentence-Based method.

Conclusion: The findings emphasize the significance of chunking strategy on retrieval performance in low-resource languages, advocating for character-based methods to enhance performance in Khmer.

Abstract: In this study, we compare the performance of four text chunking approaches: Recursive, Khmer-Aware, Sentence-Based, and LLM-Based within a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) framework applied to Khmer agricultural documents. The document chunks are encoded using the BGE-M3 multilingual embedding model and retrieved using the FAISS library. Performance is evaluated using four metrics: Average Retrieval Score (L2 distance), Answer Relevance, Khmer Coverage, and Khmer Intersection over Union, all measured against ground-truth question-answer pairs. For evaluation, we perform 5-fold cross-validation over 18 question-answer pairs. We observe the best performance for the character-based Recursive chunking method with a chunk size of 300 characters, achieving the lowest L2 distance (0.4295 +- 0.0461), highest Answer Relevance (0.8663 +- 0.0199), and highest Khmer IoU (0.6441 +- 0.0347). A paired t-test shows a statistically significant improvement over the Sentence-Based chunking method in L2 distance (p = 0.0121). These results highlight the importance of segmentation granularity and structural preservation for optimizing dense retrieval in morphologically complex, low-resource languages such as Khmer.

</details>


### [172] [Audience Engagement with Arabic Women's Social Empowerment and Wellbeing: A Decadal Corpus](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22204)
*Wajdi Zaghouani,Mabrouka Bessghaier,MD. Rafiul Biswas,Shimaa Amer Ibrahim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The Arabic Women and Society Corpus is a dataset of 252,487 Arabic Facebook posts on women's empowerment collected over ten years, allowing for extensive analysis in gender discourse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a comprehensive resource for analyzing Arabic discourse on women's empowerment and social wellbeing through social media.

Method: Collection of posts from 51,660 Facebook pages across 77 countries, processed with an automated pipeline for language identification and cleansing.

Result: The corpus includes engagement metrics from more than 267 million interactions, enabling insights into audience sentiment and social attention.

Conclusion: The dataset facilitates large-scale research in gender studies and Arabic natural language processing, available upon request.

Abstract: This paper presents the Arabic Women and Society Corpus, a ten year collection of 252,487 public Arabic Facebook posts related to women's empowerment and social wellbeing. The corpus was collected from 51,660 pages across 77 countries between 2013 and 2024, resulting in more than 267 million user interactions. Each post includes engagement metrics such as shares, comments, and emotional reactions, providing a unique view of audience sentiment and social attention. The data were processed using an automated pipeline with language identification, normalization, and metadata cleaning to ensure reliability and reproducibility. The corpus enables large scale analysis of gender discourse, social reform, and emotional engagement across Arabic dialects. It supports research in Arabic natural language processing, computational social science, and digital communication studies. The dataset and accompanying documentation will be released under request for research use.

</details>


### [173] [GHI: Graphormer over Conditioned Hypergraph Incidence for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22228)
*Yu Du,Wenlong Zhu,Xingze Li,Chenglong Cao,Jing Wang,Yukun Ma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GHI is a novel framework for aspect-based sentiment analysis that excels in fine-grained structural reasoning, outperforming existing models while using significantly fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses the need for effective models in aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) that can accurately associate sentiment evidence with corresponding aspects.

Method: The proposed GHI framework employs a Graphormer architecture integrated with a bipartite topology to represent linguistic and semantic evidence as token-hyperedge incidence relations, facilitating advanced structural reasoning.

Result: GHI achieves superior performance on standard benchmarks, showing improvements over baselines, including strong results on SemEval domains, stable evaluations over DeBERTa, and competitive scores against much larger models.

Conclusion: The findings suggest that GHI's compact structural reasoning approach is a promising alternative to larger, scale-driven models for fine-grained sentiment analysis tasks.

Abstract: Aspect-based sentiment analysis (ABSA) requires models to bind sentiment evidence to the correct aspect, making it a natural testbed for fine-grained structural reasoning. We introduce GHI, a Graphormer-over-Conditioned-Hypergraph-Incidence framework that is designed as an incidence-based structural reasoning layer built on a bipartite topology. GHI represents diverse linguistic and semantic evidence as token--hyperedge incidence relations, allowing different structural signals to be incorporated through a unified interface. Extensive experiments on six standard ABSA benchmarks show that GHI outperforms all baselines on the SemEval domains, and multi-seed evaluations show stable improvements over strong DeBERTa. Further experiments show that with only 247M parameters, GHI approaches the performance of 11B Flan-T5 based methods on the ISE benchmark. Moreover, it demonstrates strong robustness on the challenging ARTS datasets, maintaining highly competitive performance where traditional models degrade. These results demonstrate that compact structural reasoning remains a valuable alternative to scale-driven approaches for fine-grained tasks.

</details>


### [174] [IdioLink: Retrieving Meaning Beyond Words Across Idiomatic and Literal Expressions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22247)
*Kai Golan Hashiloni,Daniel Fadlon,Lior Livyatan,Ofri Hefetz,Jiahuan Pei,Kfir Bar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: IdioLink is a benchmark for evaluating language models on understanding idiomatic expressions by linking them to equivalent literal or paraphrased meanings, revealing current models' limitations in idiom-aware semantic retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge language models face in understanding idioms, which cannot be grasped solely through their surface forms.

Method: IdioLink comprises 10,700 documents and 2,140 queries, covering 107 idioms with annotations for core meaning spans, designed to test retrieval capabilities of language models.

Result: Evaluation of various embedding baselines indicates that current models struggle with retrieving equivalent meanings of idioms, often relying on superficial semantic cues rather than deeper comprehension.

Conclusion: IdioLink highlights significant gaps in idiom-aware semantic retrieval and serves as a rigorous test for the advancement of future models.

Abstract: Idioms pose a fundamental challenge for language models, as their meaning cannot be inferred from surface form alone. Understanding such expressions, therefore, requires semantic abstraction beyond lexical overlap. We introduce IdioLink, a retrieval benchmark designed to test whether models can link idiomatic expressions to conceptually equivalent meanings expressed in literal or paraphrased forms. IdioLink comprises 10,700 documents and 2,140 queries, spanning 107 idioms with both literal and figurative uses. Each document and query is annotated with spans that convey the core meaning. Evaluating strong embedding baselines (e.g., BGE, E5, Contriever, and Qwen), we show that current models struggle to retrieve equivalent meanings across divergent surface realizations, relying instead on topical and shallow semantic cues. IdioLink exposes key gaps in idiom-aware semantic retrieval and provides a challenging testbed for future models.

</details>


### [175] [Harder to Defend: Towards Chinese Toxicity Attacks via Implicit Enhancement and Obfuscation Rewriting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22258)
*Jingyi Kang,Junyu Lu,Bo Xu,Hongbo Wang,Linlin zong,Roy Ka-Wei Lee,Hongfei Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Chinese Implicit Toxicity Attack (CITA), a framework for evaluating toxicity in large language models, focusing on indirect and obfuscated harmful content.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inadequacy of toxicity evaluations in large language models, especially for the Chinese language, where toxicity can be implicit and ambiguous.

Method: CITA consists of three stages: Harmful Intent Learning, Implicit Toxicity Enhancement, and Obfuscation Variant Rewriting, aimed at preserving harmfulness while increasing implicitness and introducing surface variations.

Result: CITA-generated evaluation samples reveal a significant missed-detection rate of 69.48% among seven tested detectors, with human evaluations supporting the findings of preserved harmfulness and increased evasiveness.

Conclusion: By fine-tuning a defense model (CITD) with CITA-generated data, the research demonstrates that improved robustness against implicit toxicity can be achieved through targeted training.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) require robust toxicity evaluation beyond explicit wording. This setting remains underexplored in Chinese, where toxicity may combine semantic indirectness with surface obfuscation. We introduce Chinese Implicit Toxicity Attack (CITA), a controlled red-team evaluation and defense-data generation framework, not a deployable evasion tool. CITA uses three stages: (i) Harmful Intent Learning, (ii) Implicit Toxicity Enhancement, and (iii) Obfuscation Variant Rewriting, to preserve harmful intent, increase implicitness, and add controlled surface variants. On CITA-generated evaluation samples, the seven tested detectors exhibit substantial missed-detection risks, reaching an average ASR of 69.48%; human evaluation further confirms preserved harmfulness and increased implicitness/evasiveness. As a downstream defense application, we fine-tune a Chinese Implicit Toxicity Defense model (CITD) with CITA-generated red-team data, showing that such data can improve robustness through additional training.

</details>


### [176] [Pattern-and-root inflectional morphology: the Arabic broken plural](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22310)
*Alexis Amid Neme,Eric Laporte*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a model for describing Arabic noun inflectional morphology, emphasizing a pattern-over-root approach, managing dictionaries, and enhancing morphological analysis without complex rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve the traditional methods of Arabic noun morphology, facilitating better dictionary management for Arabic linguists.

Method: The authors developed a model that reverses the traditional root-and-pattern approach to focus on patterns first and roots second, separating formal inflection from derivation and semantics.

Result: The model categorizes nouns based on their broken plural forms into 160 classes, which expand to 300 when accounting for singular inflectional variations, and has been applied to 3,200 entries.

Conclusion: The proposed model simplifies the morphological analysis of Arabic by avoiding complex rules and providing a detailed, orderly taxonomy for noun inflection.

Abstract: We present a substantially implemented model of description of the inflectional morphology of Arabic nouns, with special attention to the management of dictionaries and other language resources by Arabic-speaking linguists. The breakthrough lies in the reversal of the traditional root-and-pattern Semitic model into pattern-and-root, giving precedence to patterns over roots. Our model includes broken plurals (BPs), i.e. plurals formed by modifying the stem. It is based on the traditional notions of root and pattern of Semitic morphology. However, as compared to traditional Arabic morphology, it keeps the formal description of inflection separate from that of derivation and semantics. As traditional Arabic dictionaries, the updatable dictionary is structured in lexical entries for lemmas, and the reference spelling is fully diacritized. In our model, morphological analysis of Arabic text is performed directly with a dictionary of words and without morphophonological rules. Our taxonomy for noun inflection is simple, orderly and detailed. We simplify the taxonomy of singular patterns by specifying vowel quantity as v or vv, and ignoring vowel quality. Root alternations and orthographical variations are encoded independently from patterns and in a factual way, without deep roots or morphophonological or orthographical rules. Nouns with a triliteral BP are classified according to 22 patterns subdivided into 90 classes, and nouns with a quadriliteral BP according to 3 patterns subdivided into 70 classes. These 160 classes become 300 inflectional classes when we take into account inflectional variations that affect only the singular. We provide a straightforward encoding scheme that we applied to 3 200 entries of BP nouns.

</details>


### [177] [TransitLM: A Large-Scale Dataset and Benchmark for Map-Free Transit Route Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22355)
*Hanyu Guo,Jiedong Yang,Chao Chen,Longfei Xu,Kaikui Liu,Xiangxiang Chu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TransitLM introduces a large-scale dataset for public transit route planning, enabling map-free route generation from origin-destination data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the limitations of traditional transit route planning methods that rely on structured maps and specific routing engines, and to provide a dataset for training models.

Method: The paper presents the TransitLM dataset consisting of 13 million transit planning records from Chinese cities, and trains an LLM to generate routes based solely on data without explicit mapping.

Result: The LLM demonstrates high accuracy in producing valid transit routes and effectively associates GPS coordinates with station locations without prior mapping.

Conclusion: Transit route planning can be effectively learned from data alone, facilitating innovative approaches in transit systems through the released dataset and benchmark.

Abstract: Public transit route planning traditionally depends on structured map infrastructure and complex routing engines, and no existing dataset supports training models to bypass this dependency. We present TransitLM, a large-scale dataset of over 13 million transit route planning records from four Chinese cities covering 120,845 stations and 13,666 lines, released as a continual pre-training corpus and benchmark data for three evaluation tasks with complementary metrics. Experiments show that an LLM trained on TransitLM produces structurally valid routes at high accuracy and implicitly grounds arbitrary GPS coordinates to appropriate stations without any explicit mapping. These results demonstrate that transit route planning can be learned entirely from data, enabling end-to-end, map-free route generation directly from origin-destination information. The dataset and benchmark are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/GD-ML/TransitLM, with evaluation code at https://github.com/HotTricker/TransitLM.

</details>


### [178] [Modeling Pathology-Like Behavioral Patterns in Language Models Through Behavioral Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22356)
*Nicola Milano,Davide Marocco*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This study introduces a framework for modifying language model behaviors through fine-tuning on decision-making tasks, revealing behavioral shifts in models based on synthetic datasets related to maladaptive patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore the potential of large language models in understanding and modeling human-like behaviors, particularly in relation to maladaptive patterns such as depression and paranoia.

Method: The researchers fine-tune transformer-based language models on structured decision-making tasks using synthetic datasets and evaluate resultant changes in action selection across diverse contexts.

Result: Fine-tuned models exhibited stable shifts in next-token probabilities, notably increasing tendencies towards negative and threat-related interpretations in language tasks.

Conclusion: The findings suggest that behavioral optimization in language models can generate consistent patterns linked to altered action selection, reinforcing the idea of LLMs as policy-based systems with potential for studying cognitive behavior relationships.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly used as computational tools for modeling human-like behavior. We introduce a behavioral induction framework that modifies model policies through fine-tuning on structured decision-making tasks: using synthetic datasets inspired by maladaptive behavioral patterns, including depression and paranoia, we train transformer-based language models to consistently select specific classes of actions across diverse contexts. We then test whether this behavioral optimization produces systematic changes in generative distributions.
  Across two architectures, fine-tuned models show stable, context-general shifts in next-token probability distributions, including increased probability assigned to negative and threat-related interpretations in open-ended language tasks. These effects generalize beyond training contexts and are detectable in qualitative completions, psychometric-style evaluations, and quantitative distributional metrics such as Jensen-Shannon divergence.
  Induced behavioral profiles also show partial specificity. Models optimized for different behavioral patterns exhibit dissociable response tendencies across evaluation probes, suggesting that structured behavioral training produces differentiated policy-level biases rather than generic distributional skew.
  We interpret these findings as evidence that consistent behavioral optimization in LLMs can generate stable behavioral and distributional patterns consistent with altered latent priors, linking action selection and language generation. More broadly, the results support a view of LLMs as policy-based systems in which behavioral constraints shape emergent representational structure, highlighting their potential as controlled testbeds for studying the relationship between behavior, interpretation, and generative language in computational models of cognition.

</details>


### [179] [Multi-Stage Training for Abusive Comment Detection in Indic Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22380)
*Pranshu Rastogi,Madhav Mathur,Ramaneswaran S,Kshitij Mohan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents a method for detecting abusive comments on social media using a language-based approach and an ensemble of models, focusing on minimizing false positives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure social media remains a safe space while allowing freedom of expression by effectively detecting abusive content.

Method: The study employs language-based preprocessing and an ensemble of several models to analyze and enhance the performance of abusive comment detection.

Result: Extensive experimentation shows the proposed pipeline effectively reduces the false-positive rate in detecting abusive comments.

Conclusion: The proposed approach allows for effective abuse detection in social media, balancing safety and freedom of expression.

Abstract: In recent years social media has become an increasingly popular tool for communication. People use it to share their ideas, exchange information, and discuss thoughts. Given its prevalence and widespread reach, social media must remain a safe space for people. Content generated on social media can be abusive and it has become increasingly important to detect such content. In this paper, we use a language-based preprocessing and an ensemble of several models and analyze their performance of abusive comment detection. Through extensive experimentation, we propose a pipeline that minimizes the false-positive rate (marking non-abusive as abusive) so that these systems can detect abusive comments without undermining the freedom of expression.

</details>


### [180] [Unified Data Selection for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.22389)
*Xiaoyuan Li,Yubo Ma,Chengpeng Li,Fengbin Zhu,Yiyao Yu,Keqin Bao,Wenjie Wang,Fuli Feng,Dayiheng Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents High-Entropy Sum (HES), a training-free metric to improve reasoning quality in LLMs, demonstrating its effectiveness across multiple training paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the challenges of obtaining high-quality reasoning data for training Large Language Models (LLMs) without incurring high computational costs or misidentifying data quality.
